ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for falafel identified in standard release; left null per ontology honesty policy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical ala carte serving of falafel consists of approximately 4–5 deep-fried chickpea patties weighing around 120 g, often served with pita bread, salad, and dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Falafel, Ala Carte — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Falafel Ala Carte (cr_id G00010670). All per-100g values preserved as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for falafel (FDC ID 174289) as a secondary cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — chickpea-based foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for deep-fried falafel found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: chickpeas (boiled, canned), GI ≈ 28–42 (mean ~32), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Deep-frying adds fat which further suppresses glycaemic response; GI of 32 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (32 × 28.96 g carbohydrate per 100g × 120g serving) / 100 = 11.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 499 mg/100g approaches the high-concern threshold; potassium at 259 mg/100g provides partial offset but is insufficient to neutralise the sodium burden.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit falafel portion size to no more than 80–100 g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted tahini or pickled vegetables to keep total sodium intake within the 400 mg per meal target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Chickpea base provides low GI (~32) and 4.8 g/100g dietary fibre that slows glucose absorption, but deep-frying raises caloric density to 514 kcal/100g, requiring strict portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may include falafel in moderate portions (100–120 g) as part of a balanced meal given its low GI and high fibre content, but must avoid sweet dipping sauces and monitor total carbohydrate intake, which contributes approximately 35 g of carbohydrate per 120 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 41.19 g/100g from deep-frying is very high; saturated fat estimated at ~5.2 g/100g and sodium at 499 mg/100g both represent cardiovascular risk factors, despite the beneficial plant protein and fibre from chickpeas.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat deep-fried falafel as an occasional food only, limit portions to 80 g or fewer, and prefer baked or air-fried falafel preparations that substantially reduce total fat and caloric density while preserving the cardioprotective fibre and plant protein of chickpeas.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried falafel and request baked or air-fried alternatives, as the chickpea base itself is anti-inflammatory but the deep-frying method is a hard disqualifier due to AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Falafel Ala Carte is a Middle Eastern deep-fried chickpea patty dish served as a standalone portion in Singapore food establishments. Falafel is made by blending soaked chickpeas with fresh herbs such as parsley and coriander, aromatics including garlic and onion, and spices such as cumin and ground coriander, then shaping the mixture into balls or patties and deep-frying until golden and crisp on the outside with a dense, herb-flecked interior. The texture is crunchy on the surface and slightly crumbly inside, with an earthy, herby, and mildly spiced flavour profile. In Singapore, falafel is commonly served at Middle Eastern, Lebanese, and halal-certified cafes and restaurants, often accompanied by pita bread, hummus, tahini sauce, and fresh salad. Nutritionally, falafel is energy-dense at 514 kcal per 100g due to deep-frying in oil, with total fat at 41.19 g/100g. Despite this, the chickpea base contributes 8.28 g/100g of plant protein, 4.8 g/100g of dietary fibre, and a low glycaemic index of approximately 32, making it a relatively glycaemically stable food when consumed in controlled portions. Sodium at 499 mg/100g is a concern for hypertensive individuals. Falafel is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, making it a popular plant-based protein option. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions due to its low GI and high fibre, but its high fat content and sodium level require caution for cardiovascular disease and hypertension management. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Can I eat falafel if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of falafel?
Is falafel a good source of protein?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010670, confidence 0.95 per source metadata). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central falafel entry as a secondary cross-reference. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous chickpea entry with documented basis. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a deep-fried chickpea preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for falafel (FDC ID 174289) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific falafel preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010670, and no exact match was found in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Singapore preparation. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source data; falafel is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients, so zero cholesterol is confirmed and naturally expected. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this food under food_subgroup 'Chicken' and primary_cuisine 'Japanese'; both have been corrected to 'Legume-based dishes' and 'Middle Eastern' respectively, as falafel is a chickpea-based Middle Eastern dish. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on chickpeas (boiled/canned) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for deep-fried falafel was found in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (32 × 28.96 × 120) / 10000 = 11.2 for a 120g serving.
